title: Type Conversion
description: Master type conversion techniques in JavaScript
longDescription: Sometimes you need to convert values from one type to another. JavaScript provides both implicit (automatic) and explicit (manual) type conversion. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for writing predictable code.

learningObjectives.statement: Understand implicit vs explicit type conversion
Convert between strings, numbers, and booleans

missionNarrative.title: The Conversion Workshop
missionNarrative.story: Welcome to the Conversion Workshop, where raw data transforms into useful forms! Master alchemist programmers know how to turn strings into numbers, numbers into strings, and any value into truth or falsehood...

sections.title: What is Type Conversion?
Converting to Numbers
sections.content:
// Using Number() constructor
Number("42")      // 42
Number("3.14")    // 3.14
Number("")        // 0
Number("hello")   // NaN (Not a Number)

// Using parseInt() for integers
parseInt("42")       // 42
parseInt("42px")     // 42
parseInt("3.99")     // 3 (truncates!)

// Using parseFloat() for decimals
parseFloat("3.14")   // 3.14
parseFloat("3.14kg") // 3.14

sections.title: ⚠️ Watch Out for NaN
sections.content:
**NaN** means "Not a Number". It appears when conversion fails!

```javascript
Number("hello")  // NaN
parseInt("abc")  // NaN
```

Always validate your conversions!

sections.title: Converting to Strings
Converting to Booleans
sections.content:
## Truthy and Falsy Values

JavaScript has special rules for boolean conversion:

### Falsy Values (become `false`):
- `false`
- `0` and `-0`
- `""` (empty string)
- `null`
- `undefined`
- `NaN`

### Everything else is **Truthy** (becomes `true`)!

```javascript
Boolean(0)        // false
Boolean("hello")  // true
Boolean([])       // true (even empty array!)
Boolean({})       // true (even empty object!)
```

sections.title: Implicit Conversion Examples

challenges.title: Predict the Output
challenges.description: Test your understanding of type conversion
challenges.question: What will be the output of each expression?
challenges.instructions: Match each expression with its correct result.
challenges.successFeedback: Excellent! You understand how JavaScript handles type conversion!
challenges.failureFeedback: Remember: + with a string does concatenation, but other operators convert to numbers.

hints.text: The + operator behaves differently when one operand is a string.
Number() explicitly converts, while + may implicitly convert.

hints.finalExplanation: Number("42") explicitly converts to 42 (number), so 42 + 8 = 50. But "42" + 8 uses string concatenation, giving "428".

challenges.title: Fix the Type Bug
challenges.description: Complete the code to fix type conversion issues
challenges.question: Fill in the blanks to make the code work correctly
challenges.instructions: Use Number(), String(), or other conversion methods to fix each line.
challenges.failureFeedback: Review the conversion methods: Number() for numbers, String() for strings.

hints.text: To convert a string to a number, use Number() or parseInt()/parseFloat().
To convert a number to a string, use String() or template literals.

hints.finalExplanation: Number() converts strings to numbers. String() converts numbers to strings. Boolean() or ! can convert to boolean.

reflectionQuestion.question: Describe a situation where implicit type conversion could cause a bug in your code. How would you prevent it?
reflectionQuestion.sampleAnswer: Implicit conversion can cause bugs when using == instead of ===. For example, 0 == "0" is true, but 0 === "0" is false. I would always use strict equality (===) to avoid unexpected conversions.
